statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
Alice and Bob are playing a game in which Alice has given Bob a positive integer $x\le10^8$.

To win the game, Bob has to find another positive integer $y\le10^9$ such that $x \operatorname{\#} y$ is divisible by $x + y$.

Here $x\operatorname{\#}y$ denotes the integer formed by concatenating the integers $x$ and $y$ in that order. For example, if $x = 835$, $y = 47$, then $x \operatorname{\#} y = 83\,547$.

However, since Bob is dumb, he is unable to find such an integer. Please help him.

It can be shown that such an integer always exists.

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $t$ ($1 \le t \le 10^4$). The description of the test cases follows.

The only line of each test case contains a single integer $x$ ($1 \le x \le 10^8$) — the integer that Alice has given to Bob.

Output Format
For each test case, print a single integer $y$ ($1 \le y \le 10^9$) so that Bob can win the game.

If there are multiple answers, print any one of them.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
6
8
42
1000
66666
106344
9876543
Output
1
12
998
7872
8190
174036

Note
For the first test case, $x = 8$, we can choose $y = 1$, and we have $x \operatorname{\#} y = 81$, which is divisible by $x + y = 9$.

For the second test case, $x = 42$, we can choose $y = 12$, and we have $x \operatorname{\#} y = 4212$, which is divisible by $x + y = 54$.

solutions.title: Solution
solutions.content:
2140B - Another Divisibility Problem
Let's assume that the number $$$y$$$ has $$$d ( \gt 0)$$$ digits.
Now we try to express the number $$$x \# y$$$ in an algebraic form.
$$$x \# y = x\cdot10^d + y = x\cdot(10^d - 1) + (x + y)$$$.
For $$$x \# y$$$ to be divisible by $$$x + y$$$, we have that $$$x + y$$$ must be a factor of $$$x\cdot(10^d - 1)$$$.
Now that we know $$$d  \gt  0$$$, we can surely say that the number $$$3 \cdot x$$$ is a factor of $$$x\cdot(10^d - 1)$$$. Thus, if we make $$$x + y = 3 \cdot x$$$, the condition is satisfied.
So, we can just choose $$$y$$$ to be equal to $$$2 \cdot x$$$.
Time Complexity: $$$O(1)$$$ per testcase.
